claim: A no-spin serve can be disguised by snapping the wrist after contact and exaggerating the follow-through to suggest spin that was not actually applied.
quote: Another way to serve no-spin deceptively is to snap the wrist after contact. Exaggerate the followthrough, and your opponent might not know what's coming.

claim: A semi-circular service motion forces the receiver to guess whether contact happened on the downward, sideways, or upward arc of the swing, masking which spin was imparted.
quote: If the racket goes through a semi-circular motion, your opponent has to figure out whether you contacted the ball on the downward, sideways, or upward part of the swing... If you move the racket quickly and smoothly through this semicircular motion, your opponent will have trouble picking up the exact point of contact.

claim: Fake motion either during or after contact confuses opponents about when contact occurred; if the fake comes after contact, the ball still carries whatever spin the previous motion imparted, and Mizutani exemplifies this by using different parts of the racket under near-identical swings so that bounce and trajectory are the only reliable spin reads.
quote: If you time the fake motion so that it is after ball contact, then the ball will have whatever spin your previous motion imparted on it... Mizutani uses different parts of the racket to contact the ball for two very different serves. The only way the opponent can reliably determine the spin is to watch the bounce and trajectory...

claim: PingSkills coach Jeff Plumb argues fake motion is more effective during the service action (elbow swinging out as the bat swings in) than purely after contact, because purely-after fakes are too slow for the receiver's read window.
quote: move your elbow in different directions actually during the service action rather than after it. You need to keep your bat going in the same direction as it normally would but move your elbow in different directions.

claim: ITTF Law 2.6.3 requires the server to strike the ball as it falls so it touches first their court and then the receiver's; Law 2.6.4 forbids the ball being hidden from the receiver by the server or anything they wear or carry; Law 2.6.5 requires the free arm to clear the space between ball and net once the ball is projected.
quote: As the ball is falling the server shall strike it so that it touches first his court and then touches directly the receiver's court... it shall not be hidden from the receiver by the server or his or her doubles partner or by anything they wear or carry... As soon as the ball has been projected, the server's free arm and hand shall be removed from the space between the ball and the net.

claim: ITTF Law 2.10.1.7 awards a point to the opponent only if a player *deliberately* strikes the ball more than once in succession; accidental double contact within a single continuous stroke is legal, so the rule penalises intent, not contact-count alone.
quote: a player shall score a point 2.10.1.7 if an opponent deliberately strikes the ball more than once in succession

claim: Adam Bobrow's signature deception is brushing the ball one direction, then dramatically flailing his arm in the opposite direction immediately afterward as a dummy follow-through to make the receiver anticipate the wrong spin.
quote: swinging one way to put a certain direction of spin on the ball... and then dramatically flailing his arm in the opposite direction right after, which makes them anticipate the wrong direction of spin.

claim: Under the visibility rule, the serving player must not take any action that prevents the receiver from seeing the ball or the side of the racket used to strike it — this is the line that separates legal post-contact theatrics from an illegal hidden serve.
quote: The serving player or pair must not take any action which would prevent the receiver from seeing the ball from the time at which it leaves the server's hand and seeing the side of the racket used to strike the ball.
